CWE-89.name: SQL Injection (SQLi)
CWE-89.problem_explanation: The application directly concatenates untrusted user input into a database query string instead of separating data from code execution. This allows an attacker to inject custom SQL commands, breaking the query's structural intent to view unauthorized records, modify database contents, or execute administrative commands.
CWE-89.mitigation_description: Use parameterized queries (also known as prepared statements) for all database interactions to ensure user input is treated strictly as data, never as executable code. Never use string concatenation to build SQL queries.

CWE-79.name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
CWE-79.problem_explanation: The web application receives untrusted user input and includes it in a web page response without proper validation or encoding. This allows an attacker to inject malicious JavaScript into a browser session, giving them the ability to hijack user sessions, steal cookies, deface websites, or redirect users to malicious landing pages.
CWE-79.mitigation_name: OWASP ASVS V5.3.1 & V5.2.1: Contextual Encoding & CSP
CWE-79.mitigation_description: Apply contextual output encoding (HTML, JavaScript, Attribute, or URL encoding) to all user-supplied data before rendering it in the browser. Additionally, implement a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) header to limit script execution capabilities.

CWE-22.name: Path Traversal / Directory Traversal
CWE-22.problem_explanation: The application accepts user input representing a file path or filename but fails to sanitize it against path traversal sequences like '../'. This allows an attacker to break out of the intended directory web root and view, modify, or execute arbitrary files on the host server's operating system (such as /etc/passwd).
CWE-22.mitigation_name: OWASP ASVS V12.3.1: File Path Restriction
CWE-22.mitigation_description: Verify that user-submitted filename metadata is not used directly with system file/API functions. Use a hardcoded allow-list of permitted files, map inputs to safe identifiers (like UUIDs), or strictly sanitize inputs using path resolution libraries to ensure they stay within the designated safe directory.

CWE-798.name: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
CWE-798.problem_explanation: The source code contains hardcoded inbound passwords, API keys, encryption secrets, or tokens. Anyone with access to the source code, compiled binaries, or version control history can extract these credentials and gain unauthorized administrative access to connected databases, cloud resources, or external APIs.
CWE-798.mitigation_name: OWASP ASVS V2.7.1: Secure Credential Storage
CWE-798.mitigation_description: Verify that credentials, API keys, and passwords are not hardcoded in the source code or committed to version control repositories. Inject secrets dynamically at runtime using environment variables or dedicated secure vault services.

CWE-918.name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
CWE-918.problem_explanation: The web application fetches an external resource from a URL provided directly by the user, but fails to validate that destination URL. An attacker can manipulate the URL to force the back-end application server to send unauthorized requests to internal cloud metadata services (like AWS IMDS), internal microservices, or local loopback addresses (127.0.0.1) that are normally hidden behind a firewall.
CWE-918.mitigation_name: OWASP ASVS V5.2.8: SSRF Prevention via Allow-listing
CWE-918.mitigation_description: Ensure that all untrusted destination URLs are validated against a strict, predefined allow-list before the server initiates a request. Block requests directed at private IP ranges (RFC 1918), local addresses, and cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., 169.254.169.254).
